heb.gloss: to reckon / to credit / to count
heb.paragraph: The verb logizomai appears eleven times in Romans 4 alone, making it one of the most concentrated word repetitions in Paul’s letters. It is a bookkeeping term: to ‘credit’ to someone’s account. God ‘credits’ righteousness to Abraham’s account on the basis of faith, not as wages earned. The forensic-commercial metaphor reinforces that justification is a divine declaration, not a human achievement.

Paul’s key chronological argument rests on akrobystia: Abraham was credited with righteousness in Genesis 15:6, while circumcision was not commanded until Genesis 17 — a gap of at least 14 years. Righteousness preceded the covenant sign, making Abraham the father of believing Gentiles as well as Jews.

refs.note: The foundational text: Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness.
Circumcision is given as a sign of the covenant, not as the means of righteousness — it follows faith by at least 14 years.
Paul’s parallel argument in Galatians uses the same Abraham narrative to establish Gentile inclusion.
James cites Abraham’s offering of Isaac as evidence of faith working through deeds — a complementary perspective that addresses a different question.

notes.note: If Abraham was justified by works, he has something to boast about — but not before God. This is the crux: justification by works would create a legitimate claim on God; justification by faith eliminates human boasting.
Genesis 15:6 is the textual foundation of the entire doctrine of justification by faith. Paul treats it as definitive and normative, not as an isolated exception.
The wages/gift analogy is economically precise. Wages are owed as a debt; grace is given as a gift. Justification belongs in the category of gift, not debt.

The chronological argument is devastating: Abraham was justified in Genesis 15, circumcised in Genesis 17. The sign followed the reality by more than a decade. Circumcision is a seal, not the substance.
Calvin notes that Moses does not say Abraham’s faith was perfect, only that it was genuine. Justifying faith does not require perfection; it requires trust in God’s promise.
David’s testimony (Psalm 32) provides the negative counterpart: blessedness consists in the non-imputation of sin, which is the flip side of the imputation of righteousness.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb elogisthē (‘was credited’) is a divine passive: God is the implied agent who credits righteousness to Abraham’s account.

Circumcision is called a sēmeion (‘sign’) and sphragida (‘seal’) of the righteousness he already had by faith. The two terms are complementary: a sign points to what exists; a seal confirms its authenticity.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Romans, NICNT (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo argues that Paul’s use of Abraham is not merely illustrative but probative. Abraham is not an example of justification by faith; he is the precedent that defines it. What happened to Abraham establishes the pattern for all subsequent salvation.

Moo emphasises that the chronological argument makes Abraham the father of both believing Gentiles (who share his uncircumcised faith) and believing Jews (who share his circumcised faith). Abraham’s fatherhood is defined by faith, not by ethnicity.

schreiner.source: Thomas R. Schreiner, Romans, BECNT (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner stresses that the wages/gift contrast is not merely rhetorical but soteriologically fundamental. If righteousness were earned, it would be an obligation; because it is given, it is grace. The distinction is absolute and admits no middle ground.
Schreiner reads David’s testimony as confirming that justification includes the forgiveness of sins. Imputation is not only positive (crediting righteousness) but negative (not counting sin).

hist.context: Romans 4 is Paul’s scriptural case study for justification by faith. He chooses Abraham, the father of the Jewish nation, as his test case precisely because Abraham’s story in Genesis demonstrates that faith-righteousness predates circumcision and the law. In Second Temple Judaism, Abraham was typically presented as the paradigm of law-obedience (see Sirach 44:19–21; 1 Maccabees 2:52; Jubilees 23:10). Paul reads Genesis against this tradition, arguing that Abraham was justified by faith (Gen 15:6) before he was circumcised (Gen 17) and long before the law was given (Gal 3:17). David’s testimony in Psalm 32 (vv. 7–8) provides a second witness: blessedness belongs to the one whose sins are forgiven, not to the one who earns it.

heb.paragraph: Epangelia (promise) appears repeatedly in this section as Paul shifts from the legal metaphor of crediting/debiting to the covenantal category of divine promise. The promise to Abraham was not given ‘through the law’ (dia nomou) but ‘through the righteousness that comes by faith’ (dia dikaiosynēs pisteōs). Promise and law are not merely different but operate on different principles: promise is unconditional and depends on God’s faithfulness; law is conditional and depends on human performance.

heb.gloss: to hand over / to deliver up
heb.paragraph: In v. 25 Paul says Christ ‘was delivered over’ (paredothē) for our transgressions and raised for our justification. The same verb used of God’s judicial ‘handing over’ of sinners in 1:24–28 is now applied to Christ: the one who was handed over for sin bears the judgment that was due to sinners.

refs.note: Abraham as ‘father of many nations’ — the promise Paul sees fulfilled in the Gentile mission.
Paul’s parallel argument that the law cannot annul the promise given 430 years earlier.
Abraham’s faith in God’s power to raise the dead, tested at the offering of Isaac.

echoes.source_context: The Servant is pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities. The LORD laid on him the iniquity of us all.
echoes.connection: Paul says Jesus was delivered over for our trespasses and raised for our justification. The 'delivered over for' language echoes the Servant's being given up for the sins of others.
echoes.significance: Paul's soteriology draws directly on Servant Song substitution: Jesus died for sins that were not his own, just as the Servant bore iniquity that belonged to others.

notes.note: The promise to Abraham encompassed much more than physical land — Paul interprets it as inheritance of the ‘world’ (kosmos), the eschatological renewal promised to Abraham’s spiritual descendants.

Where there is no law, there is no transgression. Paul’s point is not that sin did not exist before Moses, but that the law formalises the category of ‘transgression’ and thereby intensifies accountability.

Abraham’s God is defined by two characteristics: he ‘gives life to the dead’ (resurrection power) and ‘calls into existence the things that do not exist’ (creative power). The same power that raised Jesus raised Isaac from Sarah’s dead womb.
The twofold formula — delivered for our transgressions, raised for our justification — ties cross and resurrection together as inseparable aspects of the saving event.

Calvin emphasises that the promise depends on grace so that it may be guaranteed (bebaian). If it depended on law-keeping, it would fail, because no one keeps the law perfectly. Grace guarantees what law cannot.

Abraham’s faith was not weakened by the apparent impossibility of the promise. Calvin sees this as the essence of faith: trust in God’s word despite contrary evidence. Abraham did not ‘waver’ (diakrithē) but was ‘strengthened’ (enedynamōthē) in faith.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase ‘who gives life to the dead’ (zōopoiountos tous nekrous) may be a liturgical formula from early Christian worship. It connects Abraham’s trust in God’s life-giving power to the Christian confession of Christ’s resurrection.

The parallel structure (paredothē... ēgerthē) may reflect a pre-Pauline confessional formula. The preposition dia (‘for’ / ‘because of’) may be causal (‘because of our transgressions’) or purposive (‘for the sake of our justification’).

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Romans, NICNT (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo argues that Paul’s ‘promise’ language here picks up the covenantal framework introduced in 3:21–31. The Abrahamic promise is the narrative context within which justification by faith makes sense. It is not merely a precedent but the founding charter of the people of God.

Moo reads the resurrection clause (‘raised for our justification’) as indicating that the resurrection is essential to justification, not merely its confirmation. Without the resurrection, the cross would be an unfinished event.

schreiner.source: Thomas R. Schreiner, Romans, BECNT (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasises the logic of v. 16: the promise is by faith so that it may be by grace, and by grace so that it may be guaranteed. The chain is unbreakable: faith → grace → certainty.

Schreiner highlights Abraham as the paradigm of faith: believing against hope, not weakened by the deadness of his body or Sarah’s womb. Faith does not ignore circumstances but trusts in a God who transcends them.

hist.context: In 4:13–25 Paul extends the Abraham argument from justification to promise. The Abrahamic covenant was made by divine promise (Gen 12:1–3; 15:5–6; 17:1–8), not by human law-keeping. If the promise depended on the law (which came 430 years later, per Gal 3:17), it would be voided, because law produces wrath where there is transgression (v. 15). Paul then applies the faith paradigm to the present community: Christians are counted as Abraham’s children because they share his faith — faith in the God who raises the dead (v. 17), instantiated in the resurrection of Jesus (vv. 24–25).

rows.label: vv.1–8
rows.text: Abraham and David: Faith Credited as Righteousness

rows.label: vv.9–12
rows.text: Before Circumcision: Abraham, Father of All Who Believe

rows.label: vv.18–25
rows.text: Against All Hope: Abraham’s Faith and Ours

lit.note: Romans 4 is a sustained midrash on Genesis 15:6, demonstrating that Abraham’s faith-righteousness is the pattern for all believers. The chapter moves from precedent to principle to application.

themes.note: Faith peaks at 10: Abraham is the supreme OT example of justification by faith. Covenant scores 9 because the chapter is a sustained meditation on the Abrahamic covenant and its fulfilment in Christ.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.text: He chooses Abraham, the father of the Jewish nation, as his test case precisely because Abraham’s story in Genesis demonstrates that faith-righteousness predates circumcision and the law…

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Paul then applies the faith paradigm to the present community: Christians are counted as Abraham’s children because they share his faith — faith in the God who raises the dead (v…

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David’s testimony (Psalm 32) provides the negative counterpart: blessedness consists in the non-imputation of sin, which is the flip side of the imputation of righteousness…

ppl.name: Isaac
ppl.text: The same power that raised Jesus raised Isaac from Sarah’s dead womb…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Abraham: The Father of Faith

translations.text: David says the same thing when he speaks of the blessedness of the one to whom God credits righteousness apart from works:
just as David also speaks of the blessing of the one to whom God counts righteousness apart from works:
What then shall we say that Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh, discovered in this matter?
What then shall we say was gained by Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh?

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "If Abraham was justified by works, he has something to boast about — but not before God. This is the crux: justification by works would create a legitimate claim on God; justification by faith elimina…"

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin notes that Moses does not say Abraham’s faith was perfect, only that it was genuine. Justifying faith does not require perfection; it requires trust in God’s promise."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The verb elogisthē (‘was credited’) is a divine passive: God is the implied agent who credits righteousness to Abraham’s account."

thread.text: The foundational text: Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness.
Circumcision is given as a sign of the covenant, not as the means of righteousness — it follows faith by at least 14 years.
Paul’s parallel argument in Galatians uses the same Abraham narrative to establish Gentile inclusion.
James cites Abraham’s offering of Isaac as evidence of faith working through deeds — a complementary perspective that addresses a different question.
Abraham as ‘father of many nations’ — the promise Paul sees fulfilled in the Gentile mission.
Paul’s parallel argument that the law cannot annul the promise given 430 years earlier.

discourse.thesis: Abraham was justified by faith, not works — demonstrating that righteousness is credited through faith, not circumcision or law.

nodes.text: What did Abraham, our forefather, discover? If justified by works, he has something to boast about — but not before God. Scripture says: 'Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness' (Gen 15:6).

To the worker, wages are not a gift but an obligation. But to the one who does not work but believes, faith is credited as righteousness. David too speaks of the blessedness of the one whose sins are covered (Ps 32:1-2).

Was this blessedness for the circumcised or uncircumcised? Abraham's faith was credited before circumcision — circumcision was a seal of the righteousness he already had by faith. He is father of all who believe.

children.text: Abraham received circumcision as a sign and seal, so that he might be the father of all who believe without being circumcised AND father of the circumcised who walk in the footsteps of his faith.

nodes.text: The promise to Abraham came through the righteousness of faith, not through law. If inheritance is by law, faith is void and promise nullified. Law brings wrath; where there is no law, there is no transgression.

The promise depends on faith so that it may rest on grace and be guaranteed to all Abraham's offspring — not only those of the law but those who share his faith. Abraham is father of many nations before God, who gives life to the dead.

Against all hope, Abraham believed and became father of many nations. He did not waver in unbelief at his body being as good as dead, nor at Sarah's barrenness — fully convinced that God could do what he promised. That is why 'it was credited to him as righteousness.'

The words 'it was credited' were written not for Abraham alone, but for us — to whom righteousness will be credited if we believe in him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead. Jesus was delivered for our trespasses and raised for our justification.

discourse.note: Abraham is Paul's decisive test case: Genesis 15:6 proves righteousness by faith predates both circumcision (Gen 17) and the law (430 years later, Gal 3:17). The patriarch belongs to believing Gentiles as much as to Jews.

coaching.tip: 'Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness' (v. 3, quoting Gen 15:6). Paul reads Genesis carefully: Abraham was justified before circumcision (v. 10), before the law (Gal 3:17). Faith-righteousness predates and grounds every later covenant marker. The father of the faithful was justified by faith.

'In hope he believed against hope' (v. 18). Abraham faced biological impossibility — his body 'as good as dead' — yet trusted God's promise. Faith doesn't deny reality; it trusts the God who transcends reality. 'No unbelief made him waver... he grew strong in his faith' (vv. 20-21). Faith is strengthened by exercise, not by evidence.
